food_name: Hainanese Pork Chop (cream cracker-breaded, tomato-onion-pea sauce, with white rice)

food_subgroup: Breaded and deep-fried pork loin chop with tomato-onion-pea sauce, served with steamed white rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Hainanese Pork Chop. Closest parent is a breaded and fried pork chop with sauce (FOODON:03302757 — breaded pork cutlet). No exact URI available; null assigned pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Hainanese pork chop — cream cracker coated, deep-fried, tomato-onion-pea sauce, white rice; FoodEx2 A0BDF used as closest parent (pig meat products, cooked); match type is closest_parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Hainanese Pork Chop. A0BDF (pig meat, cooked preparation) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps to Hainanese Pork Chop as a composite dish.

allergens: gluten (cream crackers, soy sauce, Worcestershire sauce)

serving.description: 1 set plate (pork chop + rice + sauce + vegetables)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard kopitiam serving consists of approximately 550 g total: 180 g deep-fried cream cracker-breaded pork chop, 200 g steamed white rice, 120 g tomato-onion-pea sauce, and 50 g side vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Hainanese Pork Chop — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hainanese Pork Chop (composite dish including pork chop, cream cracker coating, tomato-onion-pea sauce, and white rice). Values represent the composite dish per 100 g edible portion. Micronutrients copper, manganese, and vitamin K were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component-level data (confidence 0.9) where HPB did not report these fields.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — white rice (GI ~72), cream cracker coating (GI ~65–70), composite dish estimate for Hainanese Pork Chop set
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Hainanese Pork Chop as a composite dish. GI of 70 (high) is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the dominant carbohydrate contributors: steamed white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) and cream cracker coating (GI ~65–70, analogous to plain crackers). White rice (~200 g per 550 g serving) is the primary glycaemic driver. The fat and protein content of the pork chop moderates the overall glycaemic response slightly. Glycemic load of 75.2 is calculated from 19.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 550 g serving × GI/100 = (19.5 × 5.5 × 70/100) ≈ 75.1, rounded to 75.2 per input record. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (478 mg/100 g); a 550 g serving delivers approximately 2,629 mg sodium — exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Hainanese Pork Chop to occasional consumption, request reduced sauce (the tomato-ketchup sauce, Worcestershire sauce, and soy-oyster marinade are the primary sodium sources), and pair with a low-sodium side to stay within daily sodium targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per serving (GL 75.2) driven primarily by 200 g steamed white rice (GI ~72) plus cream cracker coating and sugar in the tomato-ketchup sauce.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should strongly consider halving the rice portion, eating the pork chop and sauce before the rice to blunt the glycaemic spike, and requesting sauce on the side to control sugar intake; the pork chop component alone has a moderate GL and is acceptable in controlled portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.0 g/100 g), trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g from deep-frying), high sodium (478 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (42 mg/100 g) from deep-fried pork and cream cracker coating.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption due to the combined sodium, saturated fat, and trans fat load from deep-frying; choosing a smaller pork portion, requesting less sauce, and substituting brown rice for white rice would meaningfully reduce cardiovascular risk per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycaemic load may promote glycaemic-inflammatory response; however, pork loin provides anti-inflammatory selenium (22 mcg/100 g), zinc (1.5 mg/100 g), and thiamine (0.25 mg/100 g), and the tomato sauce contributes lycopene.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Hainanese Pork Chop in moderation, prioritising the pork and sauce components while reducing the white rice portion to limit the glycaemic-inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hainanese Pork Chop (海南猪扒饭) is one of Singapore's most iconic Hainanese-Western fusion dishes, born when Hainanese cooks trained in British colonial kitchens brought their culinary skills to their own kopitiams (traditional coffee shops). The dish features thin pork loin chops that are flattened with a mallet, marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, oyster sauce, and five-spice powder, dipped in beaten egg, then coated in finely crushed cream crackers — a deliberate Singaporean substitution for the European breadcrumb coating used in schnitzel. The cream cracker coating creates a lighter, crunchier, less oil-absorbent crust compared to standard breadcrumbs. The coated chops are deep-fried until shatteringly crisp on the outside and juicy inside. The defining feature of the dish is its signature sauce: a sweet, glossy, tangy tomato-ketchup sauce cooked with sliced onions, frozen green peas, Worcestershire sauce, and sugar — a completely Singaporean creation with no European equivalent. The dish is served with a generous scoop of steamed white rice. Taste profile: savoury-sweet-tangy with a crispy-juicy textural contrast. Nutritionally, the composite dish (per 100 g) provides 182 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and 19.5 g carbohydrate. A standard 550 g kopitiam serving delivers approximately 1,001 kcal and 2,629 mg sodium. The glycemic load per serving is very high (GL 75.2), driven primarily by the white rice component. Clinically relevant for patients managing Type 2 Diabetes (very high GL), hypertension (very high sodium), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium). The pork loin component is a good source of selenium (22 mcg/100 g), zinc (1.5 mg/100 g), niacin (4.2 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (0.5 mcg/100 g).

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singaporean dishes). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using dominant carbohydrate components (white rice GI ~72, cream crackers GI ~65–70) with confidence 0.9. Three micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component-level data at confidence 0.9. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Hainanese Pork Chop dish (pork + cream cracker coating + tomato-ketchup sauce + white rice) is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of April 2026. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g reflects trace trans fat from deep-frying oil; this is a low but non-zero value confirmed from HPB source data. Copper (0.12 mg/100 g), manganese (0.2 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (3.5 mcg/100 g) were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component-level estimates (confidence 0.9) as HPB Foodo does not report these micronutrients for this dish. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database using white rice (GI ~72) and cream crackers (GI ~65–70) as the dominant carbohydrate contributors; no direct GI measurement exists for Hainanese Pork Chop as a composite dish. Glycemic load of 75.2 per 550 g serving is one of the highest in the Singaporean set-meal category. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. FoodOn URI is null pending FoodOn ontology expansion to cover Singaporean fusion dishes. FoodEx2 code A0BDF used as closest parent (pig meat, cooked preparation).
